questions.question: A relation r(A, B) in a relational database has 1200 tuples. The attribute A has integer values ranging from 6 to 20, and the attribute B has integer values ranging from 1 to 20. Assume that the attributes A and B are independently distributed. The estimated number of tuples in the output of sigma_{A+B=10}(r) is ____________.
questions.to_find: Estimated number of tuples satisfying A + B = 10 in relation r(A, B)

understand.plain: We have a relation r with 1200 tuples. Attribute A takes integer values 6 to 20 (15 distinct values) and B takes integer values 1 to 20 (20 distinct values). We need to estimate how many tuples satisfy A + B = 10, assuming A and B are independent (uniform distribution).

keywords.explain: The estimated number of tuples satisfying a condition. For independent attributes, it equals total tuples multiplied by the probability of the condition.
keywords.example: If 4 out of 300 combinations satisfy the condition, probability = 4/300.

keywords.explain: A and B values are chosen independently and uniformly from their respective ranges, so P(A=a AND B=b) = P(A=a) * P(B=b).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='380' height='160' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A + B = 10 valid pairs</text><text x='30' y='65' class='sub ink'>A range: 6 to 20 (15 values)</text><text x='30' y='85' class='sub ink'>B range: 1 to 20 (20 values)</text><text x='30' y='110' class='sub ink'>Valid: (6,4), (7,3), (8,2), (9,1) → 4 pairs</text><text x='30' y='130' class='sub ink'>P = 4 / (15 x 20) = 4/300</text><text x='30' y='155' class='tag ink'>Estimated tuples = 1200 x 4/300 = 16</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A ranges 6-20 (15 values), B ranges 1-20 (20 values). Valid (A,B) pairs for A+B=10: (6,4),(7,3),(8,2),(9,1) = 4 pairs out of 300 possible, giving 1200*4/300=16.

given.aim: Estimate the number of tuples satisfying the selection predicate A + B = 10

terms.meaning: Relation with attributes A and B, containing 1200 tuples
terms.example: A table with columns A and B and 1200 rows
terms.connects: Total tuples = 1200 is used to scale the probability

terms.meaning: A takes integer values from 6 to 20 inclusive, giving 15 distinct values
terms.connects: Used to count valid A values where A + B = 10 is satisfiable

terms.meaning: B takes integer values from 1 to 20 inclusive, giving 20 distinct values
terms.connects: For A+B=10, B = 10-A must be in [1,20]

terms.meaning: A and B are uniformly and independently distributed across their ranges
terms.example: Each (A, B) pair is equally likely with probability 1/(15*20)
terms.connects: Allows us to count valid combinations and estimate fraction of matching tuples

given.plan: Count how many (A, B) integer pairs satisfy A + B = 10 with A in [6,20] and B in [1,20]. Then multiply the fraction of valid pairs by the total number of tuples (1200).

steps.title: Count valid (A, B) pairs for A + B = 10
steps.apply:
For A + B = 10, we need B = 10 - A. B must be in [1, 20].
A = 6 -> B = 4 (valid, 4 in [1,20])
A = 7 -> B = 3 (valid)
A = 8 -> B = 2 (valid)
A = 9 -> B = 1 (valid)
A = 10 -> B = 0 (invalid, 0 not in [1,20])
A >= 10 gives B <= 0, all invalid.
So there are exactly 4 valid (A, B) pairs.
steps.note: We only check A = 6 to 20. For A >= 10, B = 10 - A <= 0, which is outside B's range [1, 20].

steps.title: Compute total distinct (A, B) combinations
steps.apply:
A has 15 distinct integer values (6 through 20).
B has 20 distinct integer values (1 through 20).
Total = 15 * 20 = 300.
steps.note: Since A and B are independently distributed uniformly, each combination is equally likely.

steps.title: Estimate number of matching tuples
steps.formula_raw: Estimated count = Total tuples * (valid pairs / total combinations) = 1200 * (4 / 300)
steps.note: This is the standard selectivity estimation formula for independent attributes.

solution.result: The estimated number of tuples in sigma_{A+B=10}(r) is 16.

questions.formula_note: Selection cardinality estimation: estimated tuples = N * (number of satisfying value combinations) / (product of domain sizes), valid when attributes are independently and uniformly distributed.

questions.question:
The relation scheme given below is used to store information about the employees of a company, where empId is the key and deptId indicates the department to which the employee is assigned. Each employee is assigned to exactly one department.

emp(empId, name, gender, salary, deptId)

Consider the following SQL query:

select deptId, count(*)
from emp
where gender = 'female' AND salary > (select avg(salary) from emp)
group by deptId

The above query gives, for each department, the number of:
A. employees in the department
B. employees in the company
C. female employees in the department whose salary is greater than the average salary of all employees in the company
D. female employees in the company whose salary is greater than the average salary of all employees in the company
questions.answer: C. female employees in the department whose salary is greater than the average salary of all employees in the company
questions.to_find: What does the SQL query compute for each department

understand.plain: An employee table has empId, name, gender, salary, and deptId. The SQL query filters female employees whose salary is greater than the overall average salary of the entire company, then groups them by department and counts them. We need to identify exactly what the query computes.

keywords.explain: The subquery (select avg(salary) from emp) is uncorrelated — it does not reference the outer query, so it computes the average salary of ALL employees in the company, not per department.
keywords.example: avg(salary) over all rows of emp is a single value used in the WHERE clause filter.

keywords.explain: WHERE filters rows before grouping. GROUP BY then groups the filtered rows. count(*) counts rows in each group (department).
keywords.example: After filtering female employees with salary > company average, group by deptId and count.

understand.visual_alt: SQL pipeline: filter rows where gender='female' AND salary > company-wide avg(salary), then group by deptId and count rows per group.

given.aim: Determine what the SQL query counts for each department

terms.meaning: Employee table with empId as primary key; deptId links employee to one department
terms.connects: Source table for the entire query

terms.meaning: Filters only female employees whose salary exceeds the company-wide average
terms.example: If company avg salary = 60000, only female employees earning > 60000 pass the filter

terms.meaning: Computes the average salary of ALL employees in the entire company (not per department)
terms.example: Returns a single scalar value, e.g., 60000
terms.connects: The subquery is uncorrelated so it computes the global average, not a per-department average

terms.meaning: After filtering, rows are grouped by department so count(*) gives per-department counts
terms.example: If 3 qualifying female employees are in D01, count(*) = 3 for D01
terms.connects: Produces one row per department in the output

given.plan: Trace the SQL execution: the subquery computes the global average salary. The WHERE clause filters female employees with salary above this global average. GROUP BY deptId and count(*) then counts qualifying employees per department.

steps.title: Evaluate the subquery
steps.apply: The subquery 'select avg(salary) from emp' is uncorrelated — it references only the emp table without any outer-query reference. It returns a single scalar: the average salary of ALL employees in the company.
steps.note: This is NOT the average salary per department. It is one global value for the entire company.

steps.title: Apply WHERE filter
steps.apply: The WHERE clause keeps only rows where the employee is female AND their salary exceeds the company-wide average. This filters the emp table down to qualifying female employees only.
steps.note: Option D says 'female employees in the company' but does not say 'per department' — it misses the GROUP BY aspect.

steps.title: GROUP BY and count
steps.apply:
The filtered rows (qualifying female employees) are grouped by deptId. count(*) in each group gives the number of female employees in THAT department whose salary > company average.
Result: one row per department with (deptId, count_of_qualifying_female_employees).
steps.note: The query gives per-department counts, so answer C is correct: 'for each department, the number of female employees in the department whose salary > average salary of all employees in the company'.

solution.result: C. female employees in the department whose salary is greater than the average salary of all employees in the company

questions.formula_note: SQL execution order: FROM -> WHERE (with uncorrelated subquery evaluated once) -> GROUP BY -> SELECT aggregation. The subquery 'select avg(salary) from emp' computes a global average, not a per-group average.

questions.question:
Consider the relational database with the following four schemas and their respective instances:

Student(sNo, sName, dNo)
Dept(dNo, dName)
Course(cNo, cName, dNo)
Register(sNo, cNo)

Student:
sNo | sName   | dNo
S01 | James   | D01
S02 | Rocky   | D01
S03 | Jackson | D02
S04 | June    | D01
S05 | Milli   | D02

Dept:
dNo | dName
D01 | CSE
D02 | EEE

Course:
cNo | cName | dNo
C11 | OS    | D01
C12 | OS    | D01
C21 | DE    | D02
C22 | PT    | D02
C23 | CV    | D03

Register:
sNo | cNo
S01 | C12
S02 | C11
S03 | C21
S03 | C22
S04 | C11
S04 | C12
S05 | C21

Consider the following SQL query:

SELECT sNo
FROM Student AS S
WHERE dNo = 'D01'
  AND sNo IN (
    SELECT sNo FROM Register AS R
    WHERE cNo IN (
      SELECT cNo FROM Course WHERE dNo = 'D01'
    )
  )
EXCEPT
SELECT sNo
FROM Student
WHERE dNo = 'D01'
  AND sNo NOT IN (
    SELECT sNo FROM Register
    WHERE cNo IN (
      SELECT cNo FROM Course WHERE dNo = 'D01'
    )
  )

The number of rows returned by the above SQL query is ____________.
A. 4
B. 5
C. 0
D. 3
questions.to_find: Number of rows returned by the SQL query with EXCEPT

understand.plain: We have four tables: Student, Dept, Course, and Register. The SQL query first finds D01 students who ARE registered in at least one D01 course, then uses EXCEPT to remove those D01 students who are NOT registered in any D01 course. We need to count how many rows the final result has.

keywords.explain: EXCEPT returns rows from the first SELECT that do not appear in the second SELECT (set difference). Duplicates are removed.
Tests whether a value appears in the result set of a subquery.
keywords.example: sNo IN (SELECT sNo FROM Register WHERE cNo IN (...)) checks if student is registered in a matching course.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='440' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>SQL EXCEPT Analysis</text><text x='20' y='58' class='sub ink'>D01 students: S01(James), S02(Rocky), S04(June)</text><text x='20' y='78' class='sub ink'>D01 courses: C11, C12</text><text x='20' y='100' class='sub mut'>First SELECT (D01 students registered in D01 courses):</text><text x='20' y='118' class='sub ink'>S01→C12(D01)✓  S02→C11(D01)✓  S04→C11,C12(D01)✓</text><text x='20' y='136' class='sub ink'>Set A = {S01, S02, S04}</text><text x='20' y='158' class='sub mut'>Second SELECT (D01 students NOT registered in D01 courses):</text><text x='20' y='176' class='sub ink'>None — all D01 students have a D01 course registration</text><text x='20' y='198' class='tag ink'>Result = {S01,S02,S04} EXCEPT {} = {S01,S02,S04} → 3 rows</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: D01 students: S01, S02, S04. D01 courses: C11, C12. All three D01 students are registered in at least one D01 course. No D01 student is unregistered in all D01 courses. EXCEPT removes nothing, result = 3 rows.

given.aim: Find the count of rows returned by the SQL query involving EXCEPT on the given database instance

terms.meaning: Students belonging to department D01: S01 (James), S02 (Rocky), S04 (June)
terms.example: S03 and S05 are in D02, so they are excluded
terms.connects: Both parts of the EXCEPT query filter on dNo = 'D01'

terms.meaning: Courses offered by department D01: C11 and C12
terms.example: C21, C22 are D02 courses; C23 is D03
terms.connects: The inner subquery selects cNo from Course where dNo = 'D01', giving {C11, C12}

terms.meaning: Records which student is registered in which course
terms.example: S01-C12, S02-C11, S04-C11, S04-C12 are D01-student registrations in D01 courses
terms.connects: Used to check if a D01 student is registered in any D01 course

terms.meaning: Set difference: returns rows in first query result that are not in second query result
terms.example: {S01,S02,S04} EXCEPT {x} removes x from the first set
terms.connects: Determines the final result by removing second-query rows from first-query rows

given.plan: Evaluate the first SELECT to get D01 students registered in at least one D01 course. Evaluate the second SELECT to get D01 students NOT registered in any D01 course. Apply EXCEPT (set difference) and count the result.

steps.title: Identify D01 courses
steps.formula_raw: SELECT cNo FROM Course WHERE dNo = 'D01'
steps.apply: From the Course table: C11 (OS, D01), C12 (OS, D01). D01 courses = {C11, C12}.
steps.note: C23 belongs to D03, not D01 or D02.

steps.title: Identify D01 students
steps.formula_raw: SELECT sNo FROM Student WHERE dNo = 'D01'
steps.apply: From the Student table: S01 (James, D01), S02 (Rocky, D01), S04 (June, D01). D01 students = {S01, S02, S04}.
steps.note: S03 and S05 belong to D02.

steps.title: Evaluate First SELECT (Set A)
steps.formula_raw: D01 students WHO ARE registered in at least one D01 course
steps.apply:
Check each D01 student's registrations in {C11, C12}:
S01: registered in C12 (D01 course) -> YES, S01 is in Set A
S02: registered in C11 (D01 course) -> YES, S02 is in Set A
S04: registered in C11, C12 (both D01 courses) -> YES, S04 is in Set A
Set A = {S01, S02, S04}
steps.note: All three D01 students have at least one registration in a D01 course.

steps.title: Evaluate Second SELECT (Set B)
steps.formula_raw: D01 students WHO ARE NOT registered in any D01 course
steps.apply:
Check each D01 student:
S01: has C12 (D01 course) -> NOT in Set B
S02: has C11 (D01 course) -> NOT in Set B
S04: has C11, C12 -> NOT in Set B
Set B = {} (empty set)
steps.note: No D01 student is entirely unregistered in D01 courses.

steps.title: Apply EXCEPT
steps.formula_raw: Result = Set A EXCEPT Set B = {S01, S02, S04} EXCEPT {}
steps.apply:
EXCEPT removes from Set A any rows that appear in Set B. Since Set B is empty, nothing is removed.
Result = {S01, S02, S04} -> 3 rows.
steps.note: EXCEPT also eliminates duplicates, but since each sNo appears at most once, the count remains 3.

solution.result: D. 3 — the query returns 3 rows (S01, S02, S04).

questions.formula_note: SQL EXCEPT (set difference): returns distinct rows from the first query not present in the second. Evaluating IN subqueries by substituting the actual data values is the key technique for tracing SQL queries on small instances.
